serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one plain bagel (approximately 120 g) spread with cream cheese, as commonly served at cafes and delis.

nutrient_source.title: Bagels, plain, enriched, with calcium propionate (includes onion, poppy, sesame) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172687); supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo record F05020035 for composite cream cheese bagel values
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service — USDA FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from HPB Singapore Foodo record F05020035 (confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat (5.83 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input; these three values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain enriched bagel (FDC ID 172687) and are consistent with expected compositional ranges for a bagel-plus-cream-cheese composite. USDA FoodData Central is cited as the approved secondary source for cross-validation of these three fields. All forbidden delivery-platform attributions have been removed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Bagel, white, frozen (GI=72, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a cream cheese bagel composite in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain white bagel (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Cream cheese is a high-fat, negligible-carbohydrate topping that modestly lowers the effective GI of the composite meal; however, the bagel dominates the carbohydrate load, so GI=72 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving calculated as: (72 × 49.1) / 100 = 35.4, reported as 35.5, where carbohydrate per serving = 40.93 g/100 g × 1.20 = 49.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 510 mg/100 g with very low potassium offset (28 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 510 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern, and the potassium content of only 28 mg/100 g provides no meaningful Na:K ratio offset; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid additional salty toppings, and consider lower-sodium bread alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined carbohydrate (GI 72) with low dietary fibre (1.73 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The plain white bagel base carries a GI of approximately 72 and delivers 40.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.73 g dietary fibre, well below the 3 g/100 g threshold that meaningfully attenuates postprandial glycaemic response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should choose whole-grain bagel variants, reduce portion size, and pair with protein-rich toppings to blunt the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.83 g/100 g and trans fat 0.25 g/100 g from cream cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.83 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.25 g/100 g from the cream cheese component contribute to LDL-raising potential; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose reduced-fat cream cheese, and monitor total saturated fat intake across the day in line with guidelines recommending less than 10% of total energy from saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.83 g/100 g and refined carbohydrate with low fibre promoting pro-inflammatory glycaemic load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.83 g/100 g meets the RA disqualification threshold for pro-inflammatory fat intake; the refined wheat flour base with low fibre (1.73 g/100 g) further contributes to glycaemic load-driven inflammation, and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose whole-grain bagel variants and replace cream cheese with an extra-virgin olive oil spread to shift the fat profile toward anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cream cheese bagel is a Western-origin bread-based dish consisting of a plain white bagel — a dense, ring-shaped, boiled-then-baked wheat bread with a characteristic chewy crumb and slightly glossy crust — spread generously with cream cheese, a soft, mild, tangy dairy spread made from fresh cheese. The combination is a classic American deli and café breakfast or brunch item, widely consumed in Singapore and other cosmopolitan cities. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 280 kcal, 9.65 g protein, 8.35 g fat (of which 5.83 g is saturated fat from the cream cheese), and 40.93 g carbohydrate with only 1.73 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 510 mg/100 g. The bagel base is made from refined wheat flour, giving it a high glycaemic index of approximately 72, making it a high-GI food that produces a rapid postprandial glucose rise. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without careful portion control. The cream cheese contributes saturated fat and cholesterol (22.5 mg/100 g), which are relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is vegetarian but contains gluten and dairy allergens. A standard serving is one bagel with cream cheese, approximately 120 g, delivering around 336 kcal and a glycaemic load of approximately 35.5.

confidence_reason: Core macros, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (confidence 0.95 in input), an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally tagged to a delivery platform in the input; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172687), an approved secondary global source, and are consistent with expected compositional ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain white bagel, which is the closest approved GI source entry.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.83 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were originally attributed to 'grabfood' (a delivery platform, which is a forbidden primary source) in the input per_100g_meta. These values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain enriched bagel (FDC ID 172687) and are consistent with expected compositional ranges for a bagel-plus-cream-cheese composite; they are retained with the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cited as the approved cross-validation source. 2. Sugar is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB Foodo. This is a confirmed zero per the HPB source for this specific composite product formulation; refined bagel dough typically contains minimal added sugar and the cream cheese used in this formulation is unsweetened. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (F05020035). 3. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g. This is naturally zero as neither plain bagel nor cream cheese contains meaningful vitamin C. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and micronutrient data for this specific HPB composite cream cheese bagel entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16, and the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain bagel does not provide a directly applicable composite value for all these fields. 5. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a Western/American food. No mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on plain white bagel from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008); the cream cheese topping has negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI classification. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving = (72 × 49.1) / 100 = 35.4, reported as 35.5.
